subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Decay: Medication Modeling, Half-Life & Competing Models
stimulus:
A researcher is studying the elimination of a medication from the bloodstream. A patient receives a single dose and the amount of medication remaining is measured at several times after administration:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Amount $Q$ (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 400 |
| 3 | 263 |
| 7 | 152 |
| 12 | 75 |
| 18 | 29 |

The researcher proposes the exponential decay model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ for the amount of medication remaining, in milligrams, at time $t$ hours.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 18$ to find the value of $b$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the model to determine the half-life of the medication — the time at which the amount remaining equals half the initial dose. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\ln(29/400) = \ln(0.0725) \approx -2.624$, so $\ln b = -2.624/18 \approx -0.1458$, giving $b \approx 0.864$. For part (ii), $t_{1/2} = \ln(0.5)/\ln(b) = (-0.6931)/(-0.1458) \approx 4.75$ hours. Note: students who use a different data pair from the table (e.g., $t=3$ and $t=18$) will get a slightly different $b$; award credit for correct algebra regardless. A student who writes $t = \frac{\ln 2}{-\ln b}$ earns credit for the logarithm step.

scoring_points.description: Correct value of $b$ — from $400 \cdot b^{18} = 29$: $b^{18} = \frac{29}{400} = 0.0725 \Rightarrow b = (0.0725)^{1/18} = e^{\ln(0.0725)/18} \approx e^{-2.624/18} \approx e^{-0.1458} \approx 0.8643$ (accept values in $[0.862, 0.867]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step

required_elements: Correct value of $b$ — from $400 \cdot b^{18} = 29$: $b^{18} = \frac{29}{400} = 0.0725 \Rightarrow b = (0.0725)^{1/18} = e^{\ln(0.0725)/18} \approx e^{-2.624/18} \approx e^{-0.1458} \approx 0.8643$ (accept values in $[0.862, 0.867]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step
alternatives.correct_example: Correct value of $b$ — from $400 \cdot b^{18} = 29$: $b^{18} = \frac{29}{400} = 0.0725 \Rightarrow b = (0.0725)^{1/18} = e^{\ln(0.0725)/18} \approx e^{-2.624/18} \approx e^{-0.1458} \approx 0.8643$ (accept values in $[0.862, 0.867]$); supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step

scoring_points.description: Correct half-life — solving $400 \cdot b^t = 200 \Rightarrow b^t = 0.5 \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-0.6931}{-0.1458} \approx 4.753$ hours (accept values in $[4.7, 4.8]$); supporting work must show the logarithm step

required_elements: Correct half-life — solving $400 \cdot b^t = 200 \Rightarrow b^t = 0.5 \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-0.6931}{-0.1458} \approx 4.753$ hours (accept values in $[4.7, 4.8]$); supporting work must show the logarithm step
alternatives.correct_example: Correct half-life — solving $400 \cdot b^t = 200 \Rightarrow b^t = 0.5 \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-0.6931}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-0.6931}{-0.1458} \approx 4.753$ hours (accept values in $[4.7, 4.8]$); supporting work must show the logarithm step

parts.prompt:
(i) Using the values at $t = 0$ and $t = 18$, find the slope and $y$-intercept of the line through $(0,\ \ln(400))$ and $(18,\ \ln(29))$ on a plot of $\ln(Q)$ versus $t$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the exponential model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a) to estimate the amount of medication remaining at $t = 7$ hours. Then compare this estimate to the actual data value $Q = 152$ mg at $t = 7$. Express your estimate as a decimal approximation.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), slope $= (\ln 29 - \ln 400)/18 \approx -2.624/18 \approx -0.1458$ and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$. For part (ii), $Q(7) = 400 \cdot b^7$ where $b \approx 0.8643$; $(0.8643)^7 \approx 0.340$, so $Q(7) \approx 136$ mg. This is somewhat less than the actual $152$ mg, showing the model fit is approximate. Award the point for correct substitution into $Q(t)$ and a valid comparison to $152$ mg.

scoring_points.description: Correct slope and intercept — slope $= \frac{\ln(29) - \ln(400)}{18 - 0} = \frac{3.367 - 5.991}{18} = \frac{-2.624}{18} \approx -0.1458$ (accept values in $[-0.147, -0.145]$) and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$ (accept values in $[5.98, 6.00]$); supporting work must show the slope as a quotient of $\ln$ values

required_elements: Correct slope and intercept — slope $= \frac{\ln(29) - \ln(400)}{18 - 0} = \frac{3.367 - 5.991}{18} = \frac{-2.624}{18} \approx -0.1458$ (accept values in $[-0.147, -0.145]$) and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$ (accept values in $[5.98, 6.00]$); supporting work must show the slope as a quotient of $\ln$ values
alternatives.correct_example: Correct slope and intercept — slope $= \frac{\ln(29) - \ln(400)}{18 - 0} = \frac{3.367 - 5.991}{18} = \frac{-2.624}{18} \approx -0.1458$ (accept values in $[-0.147, -0.145]$) and $y$-intercept $= \ln(400) \approx 5.991$ (accept values in $[5.98, 6.00]$); supporting work must show the slope as a quotient of $\ln$ values

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate — $Q(7) = 400 \cdot (0.8643)^7 \approx 400 \cdot 0.340 \approx 136$ mg (accept values in $[130, 145]$, depending on $b$ value from part (a)); comparison must note that the model estimate is close to but does not exactly match the data value of $152$ mg, indicating approximate but not perfect fit

required_elements: Correct estimate — $Q(7) = 400 \cdot (0.8643)^7 \approx 400 \cdot 0.340 \approx 136$ mg (accept values in $[130, 145]$, depending on $b$ value from part (a)); comparison must note that the model estimate is close to but does not exactly match the data value of $152$ mg, indicating approximate but not perfect fit

parts.prompt:
(i) A hospital protocol requires a second dose to be administered when the amount of medication in the patient's bloodstream falls below $50$ milligrams. Using the model $Q(t) = 400 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a), write and solve an inequality to find the earliest time $t$ at which a second dose should be given. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.

(ii) A colleague suggests using a linear model $L(t) = 400 - 20.6t$ to approximate $Q(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. Determine whether $L(t)$ overestimates or underestimates $Q(t)$ for all $t > 0$, and justify your answer using the concavity of $Q$.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $\ln(1/8)/\ln(0.8643) = (-2.079)/(-0.1458) \approx 14.26$ hours. The inequality direction flips when dividing by $\ln(b) < 0$; the rubric checks for this. For part (ii), the expected answer is 'overestimates.' The key reasoning is that $L$ decreases more slowly than the exponential $Q$: the data show $Q$ falling from $400$ to $263$ mg in $3$ hours (a drop of $137$ mg), while $L$ would drop only $3 \times 20.6 = 61.8$ mg over the same interval. Since the exponential is concave up and drops steeply at first, $L$ lies above the curve for small to moderate $t$. Award the point for the correct conclusion plus any justification referencing the faster-than-linear decay of $Q$ or the concave-up shape of the graph.

scoring_points.description: Correct inequality and solution — $400 \cdot b^t < 50 \Rightarrow b^t < \frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-\ln 8}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-2.079}{-0.1458} \approx 14.26$ hours (accept values in $[14.1, 14.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup with direction preserved when dividing by negative $\ln(b)$, and the logarithm step

required_elements: Correct inequality and solution — $400 \cdot b^t < 50 \Rightarrow b^t < \frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-\ln 8}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-2.079}{-0.1458} \approx 14.26$ hours (accept values in $[14.1, 14.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup with direction preserved when dividing by negative $\ln(b)$, and the logarithm step
alternatives.correct_example: Correct inequality and solution — $400 \cdot b^t < 50 \Rightarrow b^t < \frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow t > \frac{\ln(1/8)}{\ln(b)} = \frac{-\ln 8}{\ln(0.8643)} \approx \frac{-2.079}{-0.1458} \approx 14.26$ hours (accept values in $[14.1, 14.5]$); supporting work must show the inequality setup with direction preserved when dividing by negative $\ln(b)$, and the logarithm step

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion '$L$ overestimates $Q$ for $t > 0$ in the clinically relevant domain' with justification — $L(0) = Q(0) = 400$ (both start at the same value); the exponential function $Q$ is concave up (its graph curves upward), so the graph of $Q$ lies above any secant line drawn from $t = 0$; since $L$ decreases more gradually than $Q$ (the data show $Q$ drops from $400$ to $263$ in just $3$ hours, much faster than $L$ would predict), $L(t) > Q(t)$ for $t > 0$

required_elements: Correct conclusion '$L$ overestimates $Q$ for $t > 0$ in the clinically relevant domain' with justification — $L(0) = Q(0) = 400$ (both start at the same value); the exponential function $Q$ is concave up (its graph curves upward), so the graph of $Q$ lies above any secant line drawn from $t = 0$; since $L$ decreases more gradually than $Q$ (the data show $Q$ drops from $400$ to $263$ in just $3$ hours, much faster than $L$ would predict), $L(t) > Q(t)$ for $t > 0$
